description: Classify AI system according to EU AI Act risk tiers (prohibited / high-risk / limited-risk / minimal). Document classification rationale with references to Article 6 and Annex III.
Establish and maintain a continuous risk management system throughout the AI system lifecycle. Must include identification, estimation, evaluation, and mitigation of risks.
Identify and document known and reasonably foreseeable risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights. Include risks from intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse.
Evaluate residual risks after mitigation measures are applied. Ensure residual risk is acceptable relative to the intended purpose. Document risk acceptance criteria.
Design and implement technical measures to eliminate or reduce identified risks. Provide adequate information to deployers. Implement human oversight where applicable.
Perform testing against defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds for each identified risk. Testing must be suitable for the intended purpose including adversarial testing.
Establish data governance and management practices covering training, validation, and testing data. Define data collection processes, operations, and quality criteria.
Ensure training datasets are relevant, sufficiently representative, and as free of errors as possible. Apply appropriate data preparation steps including annotation, labelling, cleaning.
Examine training, validation, and testing datasets for possible biases likely to affect health, safety, or fundamental rights. Implement bias detection and correction measures.
Maintain records of data origin, collection method, processing steps, and transformations applied to training data. Enable traceability of data through the pipeline.
Document the statistical properties of datasets: characteristics, coverage, gaps, and limitations. Describe assumptions and known shortcomings.
When personal data is processed, implement appropriate safeguards including technical and organizational measures. Apply data minimization and purpose limitation principles.
Prepare and maintain up-to-date technical documentation with general system description: intended purpose, provider identity, version, hardware/software dependencies, interaction with other systems.
Document design specifications: general logic, algorithms, key design choices, classification choices, optimization targets, computational requirements.
Document monitoring, functioning, and control of the AI system including levels of accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, and any known limitations.
Document validation and testing procedures, metrics used, test logs, dates of tests, and test results. Include adversarial testing methodology.
Maintain a record of all changes made to the system after initial conformity assessment. Include rationale for changes and impact assessment.
Design system with automatic logging capability recording events relevant to identifying risks, substantial modifications, and post-market monitoring.
Logs must record the period of each use (start and end date/time) and the reference database used for input data checking.
Logs must record the input data for which the search has led to a match, including reference database version.
Logs must record identification of natural persons involved in verification of results, including person who verified and approved.
Deployers must retain logs automatically generated by the AI system for a period appropriate to the intended purpose (minimum 6 months unless otherwise specified by law).

Provide deployers with concise, complete, and clear instructions for use covering: intended purpose, level of accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, known risks, human oversight, expected lifetime, and maintenance.
Providers must ensure that AI systems intended to directly interact with natural persons are designed so that persons are informed they are interacting with an AI system.
Providers of AI systems generating synthetic audio, image, video, or text must ensure outputs are marked in machine-readable format and detectable as AI-generated.
Deployers using AI to generate or manipulate image, audio, or video content constituting a deep fake must disclose that the content is artificially generated or manipulated.
Deployers of AI systems for emotion recognition or biometric categorization must inform natural persons exposed thereto. Implement GDPR consent where applicable.
Design high-risk AI systems with appropriate human-machine interface tools allowing effective oversight by natural persons during use.

Human oversight must enable individuals to properly understand the relevant capacities and limitations of the AI system and monitor its operation. Include ability to detect anomalies, dysfunction, and unexpected performance.
Enable human overseers to decide not to use the AI system output, to override the output, or to reverse it. Include a 'stop' button or similar procedure for safe interruption.
Implement measures to counter automation bias. Ensure that human overseers do not over-rely on AI system outputs, particularly for high-stakes decisions.
Deployers must assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training, and authority. Deployers must ensure overseers are aware of automation bias.
Declare and document levels of accuracy, including accuracy metrics, for the AI system. Communicate these in the instructions for use.
Validate accuracy against declared metrics using appropriate datasets. Report confidence intervals and disaggregated performance across relevant subgroups.
Design and develop high-risk AI systems to achieve an appropriate level of robustness. Implement technical redundancy solutions including backup or fail-safe plans.
Ensure AI system is resilient regarding errors, faults, or inconsistencies that may occur within the system or in the environment. Implement graceful degradation.
Test AI system against adversarial attacks relevant to the system type. Include evasion attacks, data poisoning, model extraction, and other ATLAS-mapped attack types.
Implement appropriate cybersecurity measures to protect AI system against unauthorized third-party attempts to alter use, behaviour, or performance, or exploit vulnerabilities.
owasp_llm_top10: LLM01:Prompt Injection; LLM05:Improper Output Handling; LLM06:Excessive Agency

description: Implement controls to prevent and detect training data poisoning attempts. Monitor data pipeline integrity and validate data sources.
Implement controls to detect and mitigate adversarial examples and perturbations designed to cause model errors. Include input validation and sanitization.
Implement controls against confidentiality attacks (model inversion, membership inference, model extraction). Apply differential privacy or other protective measures where appropriate.
Implement multi-layer defense against direct and indirect prompt injection attacks. Include input filtering, output validation, and context isolation. Test with automated red-teaming.
Assess and monitor security of third-party components, libraries, models, and data sources used in the AI system. Maintain an AI Bill of Materials (AIBOM).
Establish and document a post-market monitoring system to collect, document, and analyze relevant data for evaluating continuous compliance throughout the AI system lifetime.
Implement automated monitoring for model performance degradation, data drift, concept drift, and distribution shift. Define thresholds and alert mechanisms.

Report any serious incident (death, serious damage to health, property, environment, fundamental rights, or serious disruption of critical infrastructure) to market surveillance authorities without delay, and no later than 15 days.
When a high-risk AI system presents a risk or an incident has occurred, immediately take corrective actions including: withdrawal, recall, disabling, or informing deployers and relevant authorities.
Complete the applicable conformity assessment procedure before placing on the market. For Annex III systems: internal control (Annex VI) or with involvement of notified body (Annex VII).
Draw up a written or electronic EU declaration of conformity for each high-risk AI system. Keep declaration at disposal of national competent authorities for 10 years.
Affix the CE marking visibly, legibly, and indelibly to the high-risk AI system or its data plate. Attach before placing on the market. Digital CE marking acceptable.
Register the high-risk AI system in the EU database (established under Art. 71) before placing on the market or putting into service.

Establish a quality management system ensuring compliance with the AI Act. Must cover: regulatory strategy, design and development controls, quality control, resource management, record keeping, corrective actions, communication with authorities, and post-market monitoring.

Before putting into service, the deployer shall perform a fundamental rights impact assessment for high-risk AI systems used in certain areas (public services, creditworthiness, insurance, law enforcement, migration, justice, employment).

control_name: Deployer Due Diligence - Instructions for Use
description: Deployer must use the AI system in accordance with the instructions for use provided by the provider. Deployer must not use the system in a manner inconsistent with instructions.
Deployer must ensure input data is relevant and sufficiently representative in view of the intended purpose. Deployer should not feed data that is outside the scope of the system's design.

Deployer must monitor the operation of the AI system on the basis of the instructions for use and inform the provider or distributor when risks are identified. Deployer must suspend use when necessary.
Deployer must keep the logs automatically generated by the AI system for a period appropriate to the intended purpose (minimum 6 months). Logs must be accessible for inspection.
Provider of GPAI model must draw up and keep up-to-date technical documentation of the model including training and testing processes and evaluation results.
Provider must put in place a policy to comply with Union copyright law (Directive 2019/790). Provide sufficiently detailed summary of training data content using template.

control_name: GPAI Model - Information to Downstream Providers
description: Provide sufficient information and documentation to downstream providers integrating the GPAI model to enable them to comply with their own obligations. Include capabilities and limitations.

control_name: GPAI Systemic Risk - Safety and Security Framework
description: Provider of GPAI model with systemic risk must maintain a safety and security framework. Perform model evaluation including adversarial testing. Track and report serious incidents.
Ensure adequate cybersecurity protection for the GPAI model and its physical infrastructure. Include protections against model weight exfiltration and unauthorized access.

Do not place on the market, put into service, or use AI systems that evaluate or classify natural persons based on social behaviour or personality characteristics leading to detrimental or unfavourable treatment.
Do not deploy AI techniques that deploy subliminal, manipulative, or deceptive techniques with the objective or effect of materially distorting behaviour causing significant harm.
Do not deploy AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities of specific groups (age, disability, social/economic situation) to materially distort behaviour causing significant harm.

control_name: Prohibited Practice - Real-Time Remote Biometric Identification
description: Do not use real-time remote biometric identification systems in publicly accessible spaces for law enforcement except for exhaustively listed exceptions (missing children, specific criminal threats, terrorism).
Do not use AI systems to make risk assessments of natural persons to predict the occurrence or reoccurrence of a criminal offence based solely on profiling or personality traits.

control_name: Prohibited Practice - Untargeted Facial Image Scraping
description: Do not use AI systems that create or expand facial recognition databases through untargeted scraping of facial images from the internet or CCTV footage.

control_name: Prohibited Practice - Emotion Inference in Workplace/Education
description: Do not use AI systems to infer emotions of natural persons in the areas of workplace and education institutions, except for medical or safety reasons.

Do not place on the market, put into service, or use AI systems that generate or manipulate sexually explicit or intimate images, video, or audio of real persons without explicit consent, or that create child sexual abuse material (CSAM). Systems must include reasonable safeguards against such use. Added by AI Omnibus (7 May 2026), effective 2 December 2026.
Providers and deployers must take measures to ensure sufficient AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems.
Non-EU providers must appoint an authorized representative established in the Union by written mandate before making their AI systems available on the Union market.
Providers and deployers must cooperate with national competent authorities. Provide requested information and access. Participate in regulatory sandboxes if requested.
For AI systems used in financial services, align AI Act requirements with DORA ICT risk management framework. Ensure AI-specific risks are covered in ICT risk management strategy.
dora_article: Art. 5; Art. 6; Art. 7; Art. 8; Art. 9; Art. 10; Art. 11

control_name: DORA Third-Party Risk for AI Vendors
description: Conduct ICT third-party risk assessment for AI system vendors and GPAI model providers as required by DORA. Include AI-specific risks in vendor due diligence.

control_name: DORA Digital Operational Resilience Testing for AI
description: Include AI systems in scope of DORA digital operational resilience testing. Apply threat-led penetration testing (TLPT) where AI systems support critical functions.

control_name: GDPR Data Protection Impact Assessment for AI
description: Conduct DPIA for AI systems processing personal data that are likely to result in high risk to rights and freedoms. Integrate AI-specific risks into existing DPIA methodology.

For AI systems making automated decisions with legal or similarly significant effects on individuals, implement safeguards under GDPR Article 22: right to human intervention, right to express point of view, right to contest.

Establish unified incident management process covering AI Act serious incident reporting (Art. 73), GDPR breach notification (Art. 33/34), and DORA major ICT incident reporting (Art. 19). Single triage with multi-regulatory routing.
